g.data-narration: Welcome to Photometric Image Formation - the foundation of how light becomes digital images.
Photometric image formation explains how light from the real world becomes pixel values in a digital image - the core of computer vision.
g.text: The process by which light interacts with surfaces and is captured by imaging systems
to create digital images. It answers: How does light become pixels?

g.data-narration: The pipeline has five key stages: light sources, surface reflection, scene radiance, camera optics, and sensor conversion.
Light sources come in types: point sources emit from one location, directional from far away like the sun, and area sources from extended regions.
When light hits a surface, it can be reflected, absorbed, or transmitted. We focus on reflection models: diffuse and specular.
Diffuse (Lambertian) reflection scatters light equally in all directions. Brightness depends on the angle between light and surface normal.
g.text: Light scattered equally in all directions • Appearance doesn't change with viewing angle
where: kₑ=diffuse coeff, n=normal, l=light dir

g.data-narration: Specular reflection reflects light in a specific direction like a mirror, creating highlights that shift with viewing angle.
g.text: Light reflected in a specific direction • Creates highlights that move with viewing angle

g.data-narration: The pinhole camera is the simplest model: light rays pass through a single point to form an inverted image on the image plane.
Modern cameras use a pipeline: lens focuses light through aperture and shutter onto a sensor, converting photons to electrical signals.
Radiometry measures physical light (watts), while photometry weights by human eye sensitivity (lumens). Key difference: physical vs perceived light.
The image irradiance equation relates scene radiance to sensor irradiance, showing how lens parameters affect light capture.
g.text: Fundamental equation relating scene radiance to image irradiance:
